title: Incidence of apoptosis and cell proliferation in prostate cancer: Relationship with TGF-β1 and bcl-2 expression
abstract:
Int. J. Cancer (Pred. Oncol.): 69,357-363 (1996)

INCIDENCE OF APOPTOSIS AND CELL PROLIFERATION IN PROSTATE 
CANCER: RELATIONSHIP WITH TGF-PI AND bcl-2 EXPRESSION 
Huacheng Tul, Stephen C.  JACOBS^.^, Andrej BORKOWSKI~ 
and Natasha KYPRIANOU1.2.4.5 
’Division of Urology, Department of Surgery, =Cancer Center and Departments of sPathology and 4Biochemistry, 
University of Maryland School of Medicine, Baltimore, MD, USA. 
The incidence of programmed cell death (apoptosis) and cell 
proliferation was investigated in the normal and malignant 
human prostate to define the significance of their potential 
deregulation in human prostate cancer. The incidence of “spon- 
taneous” apoptosis was analyzed using an in situ end-labeling 
procedure for detection of nucleosomal DNA fragmentation, as 
well as the pattern and topological localization of expression of 
the 2 proteins regulating apoptosis, TGF-P, and bcl-2, in 40 
primary prostatic adenocarcinomas with varying tumor grades, 
17 lymph nodes positive for metastatic prostate cancer and 9 
normal prostate specimens. The basal level of cell proliferation 
of the different prostatic cell populations in the same specimens 
was determined, utilizing the Ki-67 nuclear antigen. Localized 
prostate cancer cells exhibited a relatively low rate of apopto- 
sis, which was significantly lower than the apoptotic index of 
normal prostate glandular epithelial cells. Metastatic prostate 
tumor cells, however, exhibited a significantly higher apoptotic 
index compared with localized prostate cancer cells. A signifi- 
cant increase in the proliferative index was detected in pros- 
tatic tumors compared with the normal gland (5-fold), and 
there was an even more marked elevation in the proliferative 
index of the metastatic prostate tumor cells compared to the 
normal prostate epithelial cells (approximately 24-fold). Immu- 
nohistochemical analysis of normal and malignant prostate 
specimens revealed a predominant TGF-f3 immunoreactivity in 
the glandular epithelial cells, while the stromal component was 
totally negative. There was a significant increase in the levels of 
TGF-P in primary prostatic tumors compared to the normal 
prostate. Bcl-2 expression was detected among certain popula- 
tions of tumor epithelial cells in a mutually exclusive topological 
distribution pattern for apoptosis. In marked contrast, neither 
TGF-PI nor bcl-2 immunoreactivity was detected in metastatic 
prostate tumor cells, despite their high proliferative and apop- 
totic rates. Balancing the prostatic growth equation for the 
prostatic tumor epithelial cell populations revealed a substan- 
tial net increase in cell number in both primary and metastatic 
prostate cancers. This loss of apoptotic control in favor of cell 
proliferation may be responsible for prostate cancer initiation 
and progression. 
8 1996 Wiley-Liss, Inc. 
Prostate cancer is the most commonly diagnosed malignancy 
in males of all ages; 38,000 men die of prostate cancer each 
year in the United States, making this disease the second 
leading cause of male cancer deaths (Boring et al., 1993). Due 
to the rapid rise in aging of the US population, it is estimated 
that by the year 2000, there will be a 37% increase in prostate 
cancer deaths (Carter and Coffey, 1990). At the same time, 
prostate cancer is often characterized as being clinically 
“silent” in a large number of men who harbor it histologically 
(Carter and Coffey, 1990). Prostatic tumors can remain stable 
for years or clinically progress rapidly toward a life-threatening 
situation. Distinguishing prostatic tumors destined to progress 
to lethal metastatic disease from those with little likelihood of 
causing morbidity comprises an inviting challenge in prostate 
cancer research. One of the predicted benefits of identifying 
and characterizing multiplc genetic and histopathological 
changes in the same set of human tumors is an improved 
method for the subclassification of tumors which might have 
diagnostic, prognostic or therapeutic implications. 
Cell death and cell proliferation occur concurrently in tumor 
cell populations, and the balance between the two processes 
determines the net tumor growth rate. Apoptosis is the 
genetically programmed mode of cellular suicide that comple- 
ments cell proliferation in maintaining tissue homeostasis 
(Kerr et a/., 1992). Abnormalities in the level of apoptosis can 
promote cancer development by allowing the survival of 
mutated cells, allowing cell proliferation rates to exceed cell 
death rates and thus leading to clonal expansion of pre- 
neoplastic cells (Raff, 1992). The apoptotic profile of tumor 
cell populations within the micro-environment of growing 
tumors, therefore, is a fundamental feature of tumor pathology 
that may influence prognosis, therapeutic response and cancer 
progression and metastasis. In the normal adult prostate, 
maintenance of tissue homeostatic equilibrium is achieved by 
the rate of cell proliferation being balanced by the rate of cell 
death so that neither over-growth nor involution of the gland 
normally occurs (Isaacs, 1984). In the normal rat prostate, 
castration-induced androgen deprivation leads to a decrease in 
cell proliferation and a marked increase in cell death so that by 
7 days post-castration more than 70% of epithelial cells are 
eliminated via apoptosis, ultimately resulting in involution of 
the gland (Kyprianou and Isaacs, 1988, 1989). Apoptosis has 
also been identified as the molecular mechanism underlying 
the regression of androgen-dependent human prostatic tumors 
after androgen ablation (Kyprianou et al., 1988). 
Susceptibility to apoptosis is regulated by the expression of 
growth factors, oncogenes and tumor-suppressor genes that 
determine the sensitivity of tumor cells to various cytotoxic 
drugs and apoptotic signals. Transforming growth factor$ 
(TGF-PI) is one of the most versatile carriers of growth signals 
(Massague, 1990). In addition to its reversible anti-prolifera- 
tive effect on epithelial cells, growth suppression by TGF-P, 
has been linked to a concomitant induction of apoptosis in 
several systems, including the rat prostate (Kyprianou and 
Isaacs, 1989; Rotello et al., 1991; Yanagihara and Tsumuzaya, 
1992). Furthermore, TGF-PI appears to have direct apoptotic 
effects on rat as well as human prostate epithelial cells in the 
presence of physiological levels of androgens (Kyprianou and 
Isaacs, 1989; Sutkowski et al., 1992), evidence that points to a 
role for TGF-P as a potential mediator of prostatic apoptosis. 
In contrast to the anti-proliferative and apoptotic effects of 
TGF-P, in the normal prostate, its positive effects on tumor 
invasiveness and metastasis of prostatic adenocarcinoma sug- 
gest that TGF-P may be involved in promoting the aggressive 
behavior of prostate tumor cells (Steiner and Barrack, 1992). 
The bcl-2 proto-oncogene abrogates induction of apoptosis 
in several systems, a property pertinent to its oncogenic mode 
of action (Reed, 1994). In vivo, bcl-2 is expressed in a variety of 
cell types, including memory lymphocytes, neurons, as well as 
the regenerating stem cells that line the basement membrane 
in many continuously replaced epithelial cells, including the 
skin, colon and prostate (Hockenberry et a/., 1991). The bcl-2 
gene encodes a 26-kDa protein, which resides primarily in the 
outer mitochondria1 membrane, nuclear envelope and endo-

USA. Fax: (410) 706-03 1 1.

 358 
TU E T A L  
plasmic reticulum (Reed, 1994). Since bcl-2 functions as an 
antidote to apoptosis, conferring longevity to cells that have 
otherwise been programmed to die, it may permit the accumu- 
lation of numerous genetic abnormalities involved in cancer 
initiation and progression (Reed, 1994). 
In the present study, the basal rates of apoptosis and cell 
proliferation among different cell populations in the human 
prostate werc determined in a series of normal prostate and 
prostate cancer specimens, which included localized prostatic 
tumors with increasing Gleason grades and pelvic lymph nodes 
with metastatic deposits. Expression of 2 proteins that have 
been characterized as determinants of the apoptotic profile of 
tumor populations, TGF-P, and bcl-2, was also investigated. 
Our present findings reveal a significant imbalance in favor of 
cell proliferation that might underline the development and 
progression of prostate cancer. 
MATERIAL AND METHODS 
Tissue selection 
Prostatic tissue specimens were obtained from patients 
undergoing surgical treatment for prostate cancer. A total of 
57 prostate cancer tissue specimens were selected from the 
Archives of the Pathology Department at the University of 
Maryland Medical Center. None of the patients included in 
our study had been treated previously with chemotherapy, 
radiotherapy or hormonal therapy. Forty primary prostatic 
adenocarcinomas obtained from patients who underwent radi- 
cal prostatectomy were randomly selected to include a wide 
spectrum of morphological grades. Diagnostic specimens were 
formalin-fixed and paraffin-embedded and 6 sections (6 km 
thick) werc stained with hemotoxylin and eosin and evaluated 
pathologically (with no prior knowledge of the apoptotic 
staining results). Each tumor was characterized for cell type, 
pathological grade using the Gleason (1977) scoring system 
and stage according to the tumor-node-metastasis classifica- 
tion of prostate cancer of Schroder et al. (1992). The mean 
Gleason score for the 40 primary tumors included in the study 
was 5.7, with a range of 2-9. Histological evidence of seminal 
vesicle invasion was seen in association with 3 of the 40 primary 
tumors analyzed. Perineural invasion was observed in 12 of the 
40 cases analyzed. 
Metastatic prostate cancer tissue was available in 17 cases 
from patients found to have palpably enlarged pelvic lymph 
nodes at the time of intended radical prostatectomy. Seven- 
teen lymph nodes positive for metastatic deposits were ob- 
tained from 17 patients with markedly elevated PSA, who had 
undergone laparoscopic pelvic lymph node dissection. The 
cancerous lesions in all tissue specimens were confirmed 
histologically using hemotoxylin and eosin-stained serial sec- 
tions. Pathologically confirmed negative lymph nodes were 
obtained from patients with clinically suspicious elevated PSA 
at the time of laparoscopic pelvic lymph node dissection. These 
lymph nodes were considered “normal” (since pathological 
diagnosis demonstrated them to be negative for metastatic 
prostate cancer) and used as controls to determine the basal 
apoptotic index in histologically normal lymph node tissue. 
Normal prostatic tissue specimens were obtained from 9 
age-matched bladder cancer patients undergoing cystoprosta- 
tectomy. Prostatic tissue samples were formalin-fixed immedi- 
ately upon removal and subsequently embedded in paraffin for 
histopathological examination. The presence of histologically 
normal prostatic tissue in each section was confirmed by the 
pathologist. Six sections (6 bm thick) of formalin-fixed, paraffin- 
embedded tissue were cut from trimmed blocks and collected 
on adhesive-coated glass slides. 
Immunohistochemical analysis 
Slides were deparaffinizcd with 3 changes of xylene, rehy- 
drated with gradual changes in ethanol from higher to lower 
concentration and subsequently incubated in hydrogen perox- 
ide to quench endogenous peroxidase. To unmask antigens, 
slides were treated with enzymatic digestion according to the 
manufacturer’s instructions for each specific antibody. 
Tissue sections were analyzed for in situ apoptotic DNA 
fragmentation using the terminal transferase TdT-mediated 
dUTP-biotin nick end-labeling (TUNEL) technique (Gavriely 
et al., 1992). In preliminary experiments, this in situ DNA 
end-labeling procedure was validated for thc quantitation of 
apoptotic cells using sections of involuting rat ventral prostate 
after castration as biologically positive controls (Kyprianou 
and Isaacs, 1988). Apoptotic staining was performed in paraffin- 
embedded sections of prostatic tumors using the ApoTag kit 
(Oncor, Gaithersburg, MD) according to the manufacturer’s 
instructions. Staining for Ki-67 was performed using the mouse 
monoclonal antibody (MAb), MlB, from AMAC (Westbrook, 
ME). This MAb specifically reacts with the Ki-67 nuclear 
antigen, which is expressed with increased intensity through- 
out the cell cycle (GI, S, G2 and M phases) and absent in 
resting cells (Go). This has been proven to be extremely useful 
in establishing the proliferation index (growth fraction) of 
prostatic tumors by determining the number of Ki-67-positive 
cells among the total cell number per section (Cattoretti et al., 
1992). The TGF-P antibody was the rabbit polyclonal antibody 
(to amino acids 1-30 of mature intracellular human TGF-PI 
[LC (I-30)]. This was a generous gift from Dr. M. Sporn (NCI, 
Bethesda, MD). The anti-human bcl-2 mouse MAb was 
obtained from DAKO (Glostrup, Denmark). The immunoper- 
oxidase procedure was completed using the anti-mouse ABC 
kit (Oncogene Science, Uniondale, NY) for bcl-2 and Ki-67 
staining and the anti-rabbit ABC kit (Signet, Dedham, MA) 
for TGF-P staining, respectively, according to the manufactur- 
ers’ instructions. The color reaction product was developed 
using diaminobenzidine. Sections were counter-stained with 
methyl green for apoptotic, Ki-67 and TGF-P staining and with 
hematoxylin for bcl-2 staining. Negative control slides were 
processed with each experiment and excluded the primary 
antibody but retained all other steps of the staining procedure. 
Negative controls for apoptotic staining (TUNEL) consisted of 
consecutive sections of each case in which the TdT enzyme was 
omitted. 
Prostatic tissue sections were also analyzed for cytokeratin 
expression using a cytokeratin MAb that specifically recog- 
nized the glandular epithelia (Amersham, Arlington Heights, 
IL). Sections were deparaffinized with xylene and rehydrated 
gradually through changes in graded ethanol. Following rins- 
ing in PBS and pretreatment with 10% normal goat serum (to 
block non-specific binding sites), consecutive sections were 
incubated in a humidified chamber for 18 hr at 4°C with the 
primary antibody. Cytokeratin staining was visualized by se- 
quential incubations with biotinylated secondary antibody 
(anti-mouse IgG), avidin-biotin-horseradish peroxidase com- 
plex and diaminobenzidine tetrachloride before being counter- 
stained with methyl green (ABC kit, Vector, Burlingame, CA). 
The results from this cytokeratin immunostaining were used to 
characterize the different cell populations within each pros- 
tatic section, as described previously (Sherwood et al., 1991). 
Quantitative analysis of staining 
The number of glandular epithelial and stromal cells that 
showed positive immunoreactivity was examined under the 
light microscope, and the relative staining intensities for each 
prostatic cell population were determined. Precise quantita- 
tive evaluation of positive staining among the different compo- 
nents of tissue sections was performed by manual counting. 
Stained slides were viewed under a 1 cm2 reticule, which was 
divided into 100 smaller squares and attached to the eyepiece 
of the light microscope. Cells were counted in randomly 
selected fields at ~ 4 0 0  
magnification. At least 1,500-2,000 cells 
were counted per prostate section depending on the size of

Prostatic tissue sections were also analyzed for cytokeratin
expression using a cytokeratin MAb that specifically recog-
nized the glandular epithelia (Amersham, Arlington Heights,
IL). Sections were deparaffinized with xylene and rehydrated
gradually through changes in graded ethanol. Following rins-
ing in PBS and pretreatment with 10% normal goat serum (to
block non-specific binding sites), consecutive sections were
incubated in a humidified chamber for 18 hr at 4°C with the
primary antibody. Cytokeratin staining was visualized by se-
quential incubations with biotinylated secondary antibody
(anti-mouse IgG), avidin-biotin-horseradish peroxidase com-
plex and diaminobenzidine tetrachloride before being counter-
stained with methyl green (ABC kit, Vector, Burlingame, CA).
The results from this cytokeratin immunostaining were used to
characterize the different cell populations within each pros-
tatic section, as described previously (Sherwood et al., 1991).
Quantitative analysis of staining
The number of glandular epithelial and stromal cells that
showed positive immunoreactivity was examined under the
light microscope, and the relative staining intensities for each
prostatic cell population were determined. Precise quantita-
tive evaluation of positive staining among the different compo-
nents of tissue sections was performed by manual counting.
Stained slides were viewed under a 1 cm2 reticule, which was
divided into 100 smaller squares and attached to the eyepiece
of the light microscope. Cells were counted in randomly
magnification. At least 1,500-2,000 cells
were counted per prostate section depending on the size of
APOPTOSIS IN PRIMARY A N D  METASTATIC PROSTATE CANCER
each section. For lymph node sections, 300-2,000 tumor cells
were counted depending on the size of the section. The index
of positive cells was expressed as the percentage of positive
cells over the total number of cells counted. Tumor cells
positive for bcl-2 and TGF-PI staining were identified by 2
independent observers, and the intensity of immunostaining
was scored as follows: 1 +:weak, 2+:moderate and 3+:intense.
For bcl-2, the strength of intensity was judged relative to the
infiltrating lymphocytes, which also served as internal positive
controls and were arbitrarily designated as 3+. For TGF-P,
staining intensity was assessed relative to the intensely stained
polymorphonuclear leukocytes (arbitrarily designated as 3+).
For all immunoreactivity patterns, a value of <0.3% was used
as a cut-off to define negative tumors.
All numerical data obtained from the immunostaining
analysis were expressed as mean values ? SE and compared
for statistical significance using the Kruskal-Wallis 1-way
ANOVA (non-parametric). A probability that the means were
significantly different was reached at the p < 0.05 level. The
correlation coefficient between the proliferative index, bcl-2
and TGF-P staining and the apoptotic index was determined
using multiple regression analysis (SAS Institute, 1989).
The results obtained from this immunostaining analysis are
summarized in Table I. As shown in Table I, the baseline level
of apoptosis in the normal prostate was relatively low (1.9%).
Apoptotic staining was predominantly confined to the nuclei of
the luminal and basal cells of the prostate glandular epithe-
lium, but weak cytoplasmic staining was occasionally observed,
possible reflecting leakage of the small nucleosome-sized
DNA fragments from the nucleus. Cytokeratin immunostain-
ing of serial sections was used to identify epithelial cell
populations in the prostatic sections (data not shown). The
stromal component of all sections analyzed was consistently
negative for apoptosis. Figure la and b reveal characteristic
apoptotic staining in a normal prostate and moderately differ-
entiated prostatic adenocarcinoma, respectively. A significant
decrease was observed in the apoptotic index of the glandular
epithelial cells in the primary prostate cancer specimens
compared with the normal prostate (Table 1;p < 0.05). There
was no correlation, however, between decreased apoptosis and
either seminal or perineural invasion among the primary
Individual lymphocytes in metastatic lymph nodes were
occasionally positive for apoptosis, as shown in Figure lc.
Lymphocytes could be easily identified histologically on the
basis of their round morphology and smaller size compared
with tumor cells. A significant increase was observed in the
apoptotic index of the prostatic tumor cells that had metasta-

sized to the lymph nodes (4-fold), in comparison with the
normal and primary prostate cancer specimens (Table I;
p < 0.05). Immunoreactivity for the Ki-67 proliferating anti-
gen was detected in nuclei of prostatic cells among both the
stromal and epithelial compartments of the normal prostate,
as well as the prostatic tumors. Ki-67-positive cells were
heterogeneously distributed among the different cellular com-
partments of the prostatic specimens (Fig. Id). Lymphocytes
occasionally infiltrating the stroma were totally negative for
Ki-67 staining. The incidence of Ki-67 expression was very low
in normal prostatic specimens, reflecting the low basal rate of
cell proliferation in the “resting” normal gland (1.1%). Statis-
tical analysis of the results revealed a significant increase
(5-fold) in Ki-67 expression in primary prostate cancer speci-
mens compared with the normal gland (Table 1;p < 0.05). A
marked increase in the proliferative index was observed in
metastatic prostate cancer cells (to the lymph nodes) com-
pared with that of the normal prostate (22-fold) and the
primary prostate cancer specimens (4-fold) (Table 1;p < 0.05).
The mean apoptotic index for the prostatic adenocarcino-
mas (n = 40) was 1.29 and that for the metastatic lymph nodes
(n = 17) was 5.82 (Table I). Although the correlation between
the apoptotic and proliferative indices did not reach statistical
significance in prostatic adenocarcinomas (r = 0.036;~ > 0.05),
a significant direct correlation was observed between them in
the metastatic lymph nodes (r = 0.482,~ < 0.05; Table 11). As
compared with localized prostate cancer cells, metastatic
prostate cancer cells in the lymph nodes have an elevated
proliferative index coupled with an increase in the apoptotic
index. After balancing, however, the apoptotic vs. the prolifera-
tive indices in the growth equation of these metastatic prostate
cancer cells, a net increase in cell number was obtained.
In view of the previously documented role for TGF-P as a
physiological mediator of prostatic apoptosis (Kyprianou and
Isaacs, 1989; Sutkowski et al., 1992), we investigated the
hypothesis that prostatic epithelial cells lose apoptotic control
due to altered growth factor expression and/or recruitment
from adjacent stromal cells and/or enhanced expression of
survival signals, abrogating apoptosis in the epithelium of the
gland. The topological distribution and level of expression of
the 2 proteins that are strong candidates for regulation of
prostatic apoptosis (ix., TGF-PI and bcl-2) were therefore
analyzed by immunostaining in the same series of prostatic
specimens and subsequently correlated with the apoptotic
Positive cytoplasmic staining with the TGF-PI antibody was
exclusively detected among the luminal cells of the prostatic
glandular epithelium (of both normal and malignant tissue).
Figure le shows a representative immunoreactive pattern for
TGF-P in a prostatic adenocarcinoma tissue specimen (Glea-
son 8). Polymorphonuclear leukocytes sporadically infiltrating
the stroma were also strongly positive for TGF-PI staining (+3,
staining intensity). The relative expression of TGF-PI in the
normal prostate was remarkably low (0.42%). As shown in
Table I, there was a significant increase in the level of TGF-PI
expression in primary prostate cancer specimens compared
with that detected in the normal prostate ( p  < 0.05). The data
summarized in Table I11 clearly indicate that there was no
correlation of in TGF-PI positivity or staining intensity with
Gleason grade ( p  > 0.05). Prostatic cells metastasizing to the
lymph nodes, as well as infiltrating lymphocytes, were consis-
tently negative for TGF-PI staining in all specimens analyzed.
Positive immunoreactivity with the bcl-2 antibody was primar-
ily confined to the cytoplasm and cell membrane of the basal
cells of the glandular epithelium in areas of prostatic hypertro-
phy, but staining was also observed in a proportion of the
nuclei of these cells. In normal prostatic specimens, expression
of bcl-2 among the basal cells was uniformly low (approxi-
mately 4%). Quantitative analysis of bcl-2 immunostaining

An inverse correlation between apoptotic index and the

p = 0.05; Table 11). For TGF-P expression, neither the percent-

According to the kinetics of tumor growth, an increase in a
neoplastic cell population is the result of imbalance between
the 2 processes controlling tissue homeostasis, cell prolifera-
tion and cell death (Steel, 1977). Apoptosis, therefore, besides
cell proliferation, comprises a critical intrinsic cellular defense
mechanism against tumorigenic growth which, when sup-
pressed, may contribute to malignant development. Our study
examined the apoptotic and proliferative activities of prostatic
epithelial cell populations in normal and malignant prostate.
Our results demonstrate that normal prostatic epithelial cells
have a low apoptotic index (1.9%) which is coupled to a
relatively low proliferative index (1.1%). This results in a
steady-state self-renewing condition in which there is no net
prostatic growth. The apoptotic index of the normal prostatic
epithelial cells detected in the present study is relatively higher
than values previously reported by other investigators (Berges
et al., 1995; Aihara et al., 1995). This discrepancy could have
arisen from differences in the methodology used to evaluate
TABLE 111 - GLEASON SCORE AND HISTOLOGICAL GRADE OF THE 40 PRIMARY PROSTATIC
ADENOCARCINOMAS RELATED TO PERCENTAGE POSITIVE CELLS AND STAINING INTENSITY FOR hcl-2
Apoptosis, in general, was histologically conspicuous (mainly
because “spontaneous” cell death is swiftly completed) and
occurred in a scattered topographical distribution. These
results are in accord with the motion that a surprisingly small
proportion of apoptotic cells visualized in a tissue section can
potentially represent a substantial cell loss (Arrends and
Wyllie, 1991). One has to consider that apoptosis is a rapid
process, as shown by the low apoptotic index detectable in
normal tissues in relation to a high rate of cell death. In the
normal rat liver, for example, an apoptotic index of 2-3%
results in a 25% loss of cells per day (Arends and Wyllie, 1991).
In the developing rat optic nerve, an apoptotic index as low as
0.25% produces a 50% loss of oligodendrocytes per day
Our findings showed a significant decrease in the apoptotic
index of glandular epithelial tumor cells in primary prostate
cancer compared with the normal prostate. This decrease in
apoptosis is paralleled by a rapid increase in the rate of cell
proliferation in primary prostatic adenocarcinomas. These
results are in accord with results on the cell kinetic activities of
prostatic tumors, indicating a significant increase in cell
proliferation in high-grade prostatic tumors compared with
normal prostatic glandular cells (Aihara et al., 1995; Berges et
al., 1995). Immunohistochemical determination of the growth
fraction of prostatic carcinoma specimens may therefore aid
histological grading in assessing tumor aggressiveness and
prognosis. The absence of an inverse correlation between
apoptosis and cell proliferation in the tumors analyzed might
be indicative of loss of the growth control mechanisms. A
balance between the apoptotic vs. the proliferative indices of
the same tumor epithelial cell populations revealed a net
decrease in the number of cells dying via apoptosis in both the
localized and metastatic prostatic tumors. Metastatic tumor
prostatic cells may have to drive the basal rates of apoptosis
and cell proliferation in a direction consistent with the 2
cellular processes neutralizing each other. Alternatively, it
could be that highly metastatic neoplastic cells are more
sensitive to the endogenous apoptotic signals than normal or
pre-neoplastic cells. One could also suggest that lymphocytes
in the distant lymph nodes activate the apoptotic program of
cell death in the incoming invading tumor cells through a
severe “bystander damage” effect for elimination of the
foreign cells. The present findings suggest that this loss of
apoptotic control in favor of cell proliferation may underlie
prostatic tumor aggressiveness and progression, a concept
strongly supported by others (Berges et al., 1995).
Our observations showed a significant elevation in TGF-PI
expression among the epithelial cell populations of primary
prostate cancer compared with the normal gland. These results
are in marked contrast with the observations of Glynne-Jones
et al. (1994), reporting the total absence of TGF-PI expression
by prostatic carcinoma cells. This discrepancy could be due to
differences in methodology that result in potential masking of
the epitope. Activation of the latent form of TGF-PI can be
achieved in vitro by heat, proteolytic enzymes, low or high pH,
denaturation with SDS or a high urea concentration (Roberts
and Sporn, 1990). In the present study, to eliminate the
possibility of hidden epitopes, all prostatic tissue sections
analyzed for TGF-PI immunostaining were treated prior to
addition of the primary antibody, by hyaluronidase. The
predominant epithelial localization of TGF-PI demonstrated
in this study is in contrast with immunohistochemical studies
that demonstrated TGF-PI positivity in both the stromal and
epithelial cells of prostate cancer tissue (Truong et al., 1993;
Eklov et al., 1993). Although the presence of TGF-PI in
surrounding stromal cells of some prostatic tumors could be
physiologically interpreted as a role for TGF-PI in controlling
stromal-epithelial interactions, such a function might not be
required in certain tumors that have achieved complete
cellular autonomy. The biological significance of the elevated
expression of TGF-PI in prostate cancer is largely unknown.
An intriguing possibility is that enhanced TGF-PI expression
might correlate with poor prognosis of the disease. Although in
this study there was no statistical correlation between TGF-PI
immunoreactivity and prostatic tumor grade, analysis of a
larger group of tumors with more cases in the low- and
high-grade histological category may yield differences that
achieve statistical as well as clinical significance. Evidence
pointing to a potential role for TGF-PI in prostate cancer
progression stems from studies in a rat prostatic tumor model
in which TGF-PI over-expression promoted tumor aggressive-
ness and metastasis (Steiner and Barrack, 1992).
Elevated expression of the bcl-2 protein in the prostatic
adenocarcinoma specimens demonstrated here is in accord
with previous observations revealing enhanced bcl-2 expres-
sion with prostate cancer progression (McDonell et al., 1992;
Colombel et al., 1993); in the present study, however, we were
unable to detect a direct correlation between bcl-2 over-
expression and increasing histological tumor grade. Moreover,
it was interesting to observe a lack of bcl-2 immunoreactivity in
the metastatic lymph nodes, implying potential differential
expression of bcl-2 as a function of the site-specific cellular
environment. Our study demonstrates heterogeneous bcl-2
immunoreactivity among certain tumor epithelial cells in 45%
of the primary prostatic cancer specimens analyzed. Of high
pathological significance were the mutually exclusive spatial
staining patterns for bcl-2 and apoptosis observed in a large
number of prostatic tumors analyzed, implying that bcl-2
over-expression can potentially neutralize the inherent apop-
totic program of prostatic tumor cell populations. Bcl-2 expres-
sion could thus serve as a determinant of an advantageous cell
survival in prostatic stem cells, promoting the critical first step
of carcinogenesis, i.e., survival of a cell with a damaged
genome. Thereafter, mutated epithelial cells thus arising may
continue to be characterized by this survival advantage, lead-
ing to accumulation of further genetic changes and tumor
progression. This hypothesis gains support from results suggest-
ing that bcl-2 over-expression occurs as an early step in the
molecular sequence of events that lead to gastrointestinal
epithelial neoplasia (Bronner et al., 1995).

Urologic Disease (AFUD) New Investigator Award to N.K.
through funds contributed from SEARLE.
